If for some reason in the future someone inadvertently alters the shape of the generated file by changing the code of swappable models, then we will know it and the unit tests will break. Versioning this file doesn't make sense because it is generated by the tests. To avoid confusion for this repo's collaborators I chose to add this file to gitignore. An equilavent version of this markup is:

```rst
To run the server as a daemon run::
```

This code makes a worker use an ExternalTaskProcess to execute a task that the scheduler has marked as `runnable`, but which has no implemented `run` method (and therefore is assumed to be an ExternalTask).

An ExternalTaskProcess is very similiar to a TaskProcess, but instead of calling the task's `run` method, it checks its `complete` method and returns a status based on that. If the task's `complete` method returns `False`, the task is marked as FAILED. The scheduler will then reschedule it later, subject to the normal retry logic (`disable-num-failures` especially applies here). 

So no, that code doesn't mean it will only be run once at the start of the pipeline (perhaps you're thinking of `check_complete` in worker.py?). The ExternalTaskProcess will be re-evaluated every time the task is given to a worker to execute.

Also, Python 2.6 wants attribute names to be bytestrings and discards them if they are unicode strings (as they are when loaded from the model). Fixed both issues (although IMO Python 2.6 should not be accommodated).

I make this change for 3 reasons:

1. In the project before this commit, there is no difference between `set(PushInfo._flag_map.keys()) & set(FetchInfo._flag_map.keys())` and `set(FetchInfo._flag_map.keys())`.
`PushInfo._flag_map.keys() == ['X', '-', '*', '+', ' ', '=', '!']` and `FetchInfo._flag_map.keys() == ['!', '+', '-', '*', '=', ' ']`, and these two `_flag_map` are considered unchangable.

2. It seems that there is no need to make an intersection between Push and Fetch.
The only two usages of this function is `fetch` and `pull`. As the function (named `_get_fetch_info_from_stderr`) has the word `fetch` in its name, its duty is to *parse data from **fetch** messages*, and thus should only use `FetchInfo._flags_map`.
So, I think use `set(FetchInfo._flag_map.keys())` is just the right way.

3. Push command has no 't'.
Accoding to the documents of [git-fetch](https://github.com/git/git/blob/2cb040baa6fcb8a6314a54933cbcb4d3fcb60401/Documentation/git-fetch.txt#L120) and [git-push](https://github.com/git/git/blob/2cb040baa6fcb8a6314a54933cbcb4d3fcb60401/Documentation/git-push.txt#L321), fetch has more command than push now, make an intersection will make this function loss some types of records.

Since you would never have an async client and sync client in the same application, I think it makes more sense to name this `tchannel.sync.TChannel`. The client code would look like so:

```
from tchannel.sync import TChannel

tchannel = TChannel(...)
```

When we move it out into it's own package, it would just be:

```
from tchannel_sync import TChannel

tchannel = TChannel()
```

From the time you have a tchannel instance on, everything is the same, so the import path is really the only thing that matters.

> Out of curiosity I added this question on SO.

I kinda have answer to that: Loggers are composed in hierarchy, separated by dot syntax. `None` or empty string is a root logger. `'jira'` is a logger which has a root logger as parent. `'jira.utils'` would be another logger (namespace) which will have "jira" logger as a parent. By default all sub loggers propagate their messages to their parents, unless configured not to do so.

`__package__` is a good candidate, but it will create another logger namespace when used in sub packages. (Might not be always desirable.)

These two documents should have more info:  
https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging-cookbook.html
https://docs.python.org/3/howto/logging.html

Right. The indexes aren't into the JSON-encoded utf8 byte string - they're into a string encoded in the specified encoding. A python unicode object is encoded in UTF32  (assuming the python interpreter was compiled in wide mode), which is how each code point is one index. So by specifying `encoding=UTF32`, the returned offsets will correspond to the python `unicode` object, which treats every code point as one index (rather than, say, UTF16, where triple-byte characters will occupy two indices). The reason I'm suggesting UTF32 be the default is _so that_ the user doesn't have to think about the encoding, since their string is probably already UTF32 (assuming they're using unicode objects, rather than python2 strings).

Then there's the issue that, depending on whether your python interpreter was compiled in [wide or narrow mode](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13095499/unicode-in-python-just-utf-16), the unicode object might actually be UTF16.

Also note, if you're writing an app where you eventually pass the offsets to javascript (which encodes in UTF16), you'll need the offsets for UTF16 rather than UTF32. It all depends on what string you're going to be applying the offsets to. But if it's going to be applied to python `unicode` objects, it should be either UTF32 or UTF16, depending on `sys.maxunicode`

No polymorphism here. In the code
```ts
const stuff = {one: 1, two: 2, three: 3};
const {one} = stuff;  // A
const {two: dos} = stuff;  // B
export const {three: tres} = stuff;  // C
```
the last three lines are equivalent to
```ts
const one = stuff.one;
const dos = stuff.two;
export const tres = stuff.three;
```

The names of the exported interface are `getString`, `setString`, `getStringInitializer`, and `getStringObserver`.

These are used when someone writes `import {getStringInitializer, getStringObserver} from '../tf-storage/storage.js'` (or `import * as storage from '../tf-storage/storage.js'`, etc.).

Relevant MDN page: [Destructuring assignment § Assigning to new variable names](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Destructuring_assignment#Assigning_to_new_variable_names).

Pat had the same (or a similar) idea when we whiteboarded this last week. Here are my thoughts:

It is possible to implement refresh in terms of both `OpRead` and `OpCreate` and friends. In both cases, however, it is difficult to implement https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-terraform/issues/57, because the step generator must know the results of the refresh before deciding what kind of step to generate for the resource itself. This is in contrast to the step generator's normal mode of operation, where steps are generated using only the knowledge of incoming new inputs and existing old inputs. Therefore, this implementation strategy is less of a question about implementation efficiency and correctness but more of a question of software engineering, to steal Pat's quote.

I lean towards having small, composable steps whose semantics are small over larger steps whose semantics are more complex. The reason for this is that it pushes complexity away from step application and towards the step generator, where we can centralize this logic in one place that can be tested as a single unit. If the outputs of the step generator (steps) are small and have well-known and simple semantics, in my mind it is easier to reason about the correctness of a plan as a whole because it is clear just by looking at the steps themselves (and not their inputs) what a particular step will do.

Having `OpCreate`, `OpUpdate`, `OpCreateReplacement` change semantics depending on whether or not their target(s) are `External` flies a little bit in the face of that tendency I have. When looking at a series of steps generated by the step generator, one would also have to know the target resources (whether or not they were `External`) to fully understand the semantics of the step.

That said, the fact that `OpDelete` handles both `External` and non-`External` resources in this PR is a contradiction to everything I have just said. I actually did have a `read-delete` step in a previous version of this PR. Here's how I justify it, though: the primitives of Pulumi are read, update, create, and delete. All operations can be phrased in terms of those four operations. In my mind it makes sense to conflate `OpDelete` with the deletion of an external resource, because conceptually we're still *deleting* - we're just removing something from a snapshot. However, in my mind it does *not* make sense to conflate `read` and `create`, since fundamentally those are two very different operations that bottom out at different provider operations.
